subject: Physics
q: A student in the laboratory measures thickness of a wire using screw gauge. The readings are <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>22</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mm</mi><mo>,</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>23</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mm</mi><mo>,</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>19</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mm</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>20</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mm</mi></math>. The percentage error is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mi>x</mi><mn>121</mn></mfrac><mo>%</mo></math>. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> is _____ .
question: A student in the laboratory measures thickness of a wire using screw gauge. The readings are <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>22</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mm</mi><mo>,</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>23</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mm</mi><mo>,</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>19</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mm</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>20</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mm</mi></math>. The percentage error is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mi>x</mi><mn>121</mn></mfrac><mo>%</mo></math>. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> is _____ .
solution:
The average value of the readings is xavg=1.19+1.20+1.22+1.234=1.21

Now, absolute error in measurement Δxavg=0.02+0.01+0.01+0.024=0.064=0.015

Percentage error in measurement is =0.0151.21×100%=150121%

So, the value of x=150.

subject: Physics
q:
When both jaws of vernier callipers touch each other, zero mark of the vernier scale is right to zero mark of main scale, $4^{\text {th }}$ mark on vernier scale coincides with certain mark on the main scale. While measuring the length of a cylinder, observer observes 15 divisions on main scale and $5^{\text {th }}$ division of vernier scale coincides with a main scale division. Measured length of cylinder is $\_\_\_\_$ mm.
(Least count of Vernier calliper $=0.1 \mathrm{~mm}$)
question:
When both jaws of vernier callipers touch each other, zero mark of the vernier scale is right to zero mark of main scale, $4^{\text {th }}$ mark on vernier scale coincides with certain mark on the main scale. While measuring the length of a cylinder, observer observes 15 divisions on main scale and $5^{\text {th }}$ division of vernier scale coincides with a main scale division. Measured length of cylinder is $\_\_\_\_$ mm.
(Least count of Vernier calliper $=0.1 \mathrm{~mm}$)
solution:
Given Least Count ($LC$) = $0.1$ mm.
When jaws touch, the zero of the vernier scale is to the right of the main scale zero, indicating a positive zero error.
Zero error ($e$) = $+ (n \times LC)$, where $n$ is the coinciding vernier division.
$e = +(4 \times 0.1) = +0.4$ mm.
While measuring the cylinder:
Main Scale Reading ($MSR$) = $15$ divisions.
Assuming $1$ MSD = $1$ mm (standard for $LC = 0.1$ mm), $MSR = 15$ mm.
Vernier Scale Reading ($VSR$) = $5 \times LC = 5 \times 0.1 = 0.5$ mm.
Observed Reading = $MSR + VSR = 15 + 0.5 = 15.5$ mm.
True Measured Length = Observed Reading $-$ Zero Error
True Length = $15.5 - 0.4 = 15.1$ mm.

subject: Physics
q: In a meter bridge, the wire of length 1 m has a non-uniform cross-section such that, the variation dRdl of its resistance R with length l is dRdl∝1l. Two equal resistances are connected as shown in the figure. The galvanometer has zero deflection when the jockey is at point P. What is the length AP?
question: In a meter bridge, the wire of length 1 m has a non-uniform cross-section such that, the variation dRdl of its resistance R with length l is dRdl∝1l. Two equal resistances are connected as shown in the figure. The galvanometer has zero deflection when the jockey is at point P. What is the length AP?
solution:
According to question meter bridge have non-uniform wire, where its resistance has a variation with length as dRdl∝1l 

dRdl=kl  ...1

, where k is proportionality constant
Let resistance of the left part AP and right part PB of the wire is RAP , RPB respectively.

From balanced condition of wheat stone bridge is:

PQ=RS

So, from the above condition for given meter bridge is

R'R'=RApRpB  ⇒RAp = RpB  ...2

Now from equation 1, on integrating  ∫dR=k∫dll  ...3

 for AP part of the wire of length extends from o to l from the equation 3

RAP =∫0RAP∫dR=k∫0ldll =k∫0l l-12 dl =k l-12+10l-12+1⇒RAP =2kl

Now, for PB part of the wire of length extends from l to 1 m, from the equation 3

RPB =∫0RPBdR=k∫l1dll =k∫l1 l-12 dl =k l-12+1l1-12+1⇒RPB =2k1-l 

From equation 2 RAP=RPB
∴ 2l=21-l⇒ l=1-l⇒l=12  ⇒l=14=0.25 m

subject: Physics
q: The least count of the main scale of a screw gauge is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mi> </mi><mi>mm</mi><mo>.</mo></math> The minimum number of divisions on its circular scale required to measure <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi>μm</mi></math> diameter of a wire is:
question: The least count of the main scale of a screw gauge is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mi> </mi><mi>mm</mi><mo>.</mo></math> The minimum number of divisions on its circular scale required to measure <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi>μm</mi></math> diameter of a wire is:
solution:
Least count of screw gauge =5 μm

L.C=pitchno. of div on cirxular  scale

5 μm=1 mmN

N=200

subject: Physics
q:
Given below are two statements :
Statement I : In a vernier callipers, one vernier scale division is always smaller than one main scale division.
Statement II : The vernier constant is given by one main scale division multiplied by the number of vernier scale divisions.
In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below.
question:
Given below are two statements :
Statement I : In a vernier callipers, one vernier scale division is always smaller than one main scale division.
Statement II : The vernier constant is given by one main scale division multiplied by the number of vernier scale divisions.
In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below.
options: Statement I is true but Statement II is false
Statement I is false but Statement II is true
Both Statement I and Statement II are false
Both Statement I and Statement II are true
solution: In general one vernier scale division is smaller than one main scale division but in some modified cases it may be not correct. Also least count is given by one main scale division / number of vernier scale division for normal vernier calliper.

subject: Physics
q:
Given below are two statements: one is labelled as Assertion(A) and the other is labelled as Reason (R).
Assertion (A) : In Vernier calliper if positive zero error exists, then while taking measurements, the reading taken will be more than the actual reading.
Reason (R) : The zero error in Vernier Calliper might have happened due to manufacturing defect or due to rough handling.
In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below :
question:
Given below are two statements: one is labelled as Assertion(A) and the other is labelled as Reason (R).
Assertion (A) : In Vernier calliper if positive zero error exists, then while taking measurements, the reading taken will be more than the actual reading.
Reason (R) : The zero error in Vernier Calliper might have happened due to manufacturing defect or due to rough handling.
In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below :
options: Both (A) and (R) are correct and (R) is the correct explanation of (A)
Both (A) and (R) are correct but (R) is not the correct explanation of (A)
solution:
If a positive zero error exists in a vernier callipers, it means that the instrument shows a reading greater than zero even when the jaws are closed and there is no object being measured. In such a case, when actual measurements are taken, the readings will be more than the actual values.

The zero error in Vernier callipers can indeed occur due to manufacturing defects or rough handling. Manufacturing defects may lead to misalignments or inaccuracies in the instrument, while rough handling can result in damage that affects the calibration of the callipers.

Hence, both (A) and (R) are correct, but (R) is not the correct explanation of (A)

subject: Physics
q: A load of mass <math><mi>M</mi><mi> </mi><mi>k</mi><mi>g</mi></math> is suspended from a steel wire of length <math><mn>2</mn><mi>m</mi></math> and radius <math><mn>1.0</mn><mi> </mi><mi>m</mi><mi>m</mi></math> in Searle's apparatus experiment. The increase in length produced in the wire is <math><mn>4.0</mn><mi> </mi><mi>m</mi><mi>m</mi><mo>.</mo></math> Now the load is fully immersed in a liquid of relative density <math><mn>2</mn><mo>.</mo></math> The relative density of the material of load is <math><mn>8</mn><mo>.</mo></math> The new value of increase in length of the steel wire is:
question: A load of mass <math><mi>M</mi><mi> </mi><mi>k</mi><mi>g</mi></math> is suspended from a steel wire of length <math><mn>2</mn><mi>m</mi></math> and radius <math><mn>1.0</mn><mi> </mi><mi>m</mi><mi>m</mi></math> in Searle's apparatus experiment. The increase in length produced in the wire is <math><mn>4.0</mn><mi> </mi><mi>m</mi><mi>m</mi><mo>.</mo></math> Now the load is fully immersed in a liquid of relative density <math><mn>2</mn><mo>.</mo></math> The relative density of the material of load is <math><mn>8</mn><mo>.</mo></math> The new value of increase in length of the steel wire is:
solution:
δ=ρ0vg×LAy

δ′=ρ0-ρLvg×LAy⇒δ^′δ=ρ0-ρLρ0=8-28

δ′=3 mm

subject: Physics
q: The one division of main scale of vernier callipers reads <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mm</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn></math> divisions of Vernier scale is equal to the <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>9</mn></math> divisions on main scale. When the two jaws of the instrument touch each other the zero of the Vernier lies to the right of zero of the main scale and its fourth division coincides with a main scale division. When a spherical bob is tightly placed between the two jaws, the zero of the Vernier scale lies in between <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mn>6</mn><mrow><mi>th</mi><mo> </mo></mrow></msup></math> Vernier division coincides with a main scale division. The diameter of the bob will be _____ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math>.
question: The one division of main scale of vernier callipers reads <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mm</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn></math> divisions of Vernier scale is equal to the <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>9</mn></math> divisions on main scale. When the two jaws of the instrument touch each other the zero of the Vernier lies to the right of zero of the main scale and its fourth division coincides with a main scale division. When a spherical bob is tightly placed between the two jaws, the zero of the Vernier scale lies in between <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mn>6</mn><mrow><mi>th</mi><mo> </mo></mrow></msup></math> Vernier division coincides with a main scale division. The diameter of the bob will be _____ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math>.
solution:
Given,

1 MSD=1 mm

10 VSD=9 MSD

Then, 1 VSD=910MSD=0.9 MSD

Least count of Vernier calliper is L.C. =0.1 mm=0.01 cm

Positive zero error=0.4 mm =0.04 cm

Observed reading of diameter of spherical bob=4.1 cm+6×0.01=4.16 cm

Real reading of diameter of bob=4.16-0.04=4.12 cm =412×10-2 cm

q:
The pitch and the number of divisions, on the circular scale, for a given screw gauge are 0.5 mm and 100 respectively. When the screw gauge is fully tightened without any object, the zero of its circular scale lies 3 divisions below the mean line.

The readings of the main scale and the circular scale, for a thin sheet, are 5.5 mm and 48 respectively, the thickness of this sheet is:
question:
The pitch and the number of divisions, on the circular scale, for a given screw gauge are 0.5 mm and 100 respectively. When the screw gauge is fully tightened without any object, the zero of its circular scale lies 3 divisions below the mean line.

The readings of the main scale and the circular scale, for a thin sheet, are 5.5 mm and 48 respectively, the thickness of this sheet is:
solution:
least count =0.5 mm100=0.005 mm

Reading =5.5 mm+48-3×0.005 mm

=5.725 mm.

subject: Physics
q:
The vernier scale used for measurement has a positive zero error of 0.2 mm. If while taking a measurement it was noted that 0 on the vernier scale lies between 8.5 cm and 8.6 cm. Vernier coincidence is 6, then the correct value of measurement is cm.

(least count=0.01 cm)
question:
The vernier scale used for measurement has a positive zero error of 0.2 mm. If while taking a measurement it was noted that 0 on the vernier scale lies between 8.5 cm and 8.6 cm. Vernier coincidence is 6, then the correct value of measurement is cm.

(least count=0.01 cm)
solution:
Positive zero error =0.2 mm

Main scale reading =8.5 cm

Vernier scale reading=6×0.01=0.06 cm

Final reading=8.5+0.06-0.02=8.54 cm

Upon one complete rotation of the circular scale, a displacement of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mi>mm</mi></math> is noticed on the pitch scale. The nature of zero error involved and the lest count of the screw gauge, are respectively:
question: A screw gauge has 50 divisions on its circular scale. The circular scale is 4 units ahead of the pitch scale marking, prior to use. Upon one complete rotation of the circular scale, a displacement of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mi>mm</mi></math> is noticed on the pitch scale. The nature of zero error involved and the lest count of the screw gauge, are respectively:

subject: Physics
q: The diameter of a sphere is measured using a vernier caliper whose 9 divisions of main scale are equal to 10 divisions of vernier scale. The shortest division on the main scale is equal to $1 \mathrm{~mm}$. The main scale reading is $2 \mathrm{~cm}$ and second division of vernier scale coincides with a division on main scale. If mass of the sphere is 8.635 g, the density of the sphere is:
question: The diameter of a sphere is measured using a vernier caliper whose 9 divisions of main scale are equal to 10 divisions of vernier scale. The shortest division on the main scale is equal to $1 \mathrm{~mm}$. The main scale reading is $2 \mathrm{~cm}$ and second division of vernier scale coincides with a division on main scale. If mass of the sphere is 8.635 g, the density of the sphere is:
solution: Given $9 \mathrm{MSD}=10 \mathrm{VSD}$ $\operatorname{mass}=8.635 \mathrm{~g}$ $\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{LC}=1 \mathrm{MSD}-1 \mathrm{VSD} \\ & \mathrm{LC}=1 \mathrm{MSD}-\frac{9}{10} \mathrm{MSD} \\ & \mathrm{LC}=\frac{1}{10} \mathrm{MSD} \\ & \mathrm{LC}=0.01 \mathrm{~cm}\end{aligned}$ Reading of diameter $=\mathrm{MSR}+\mathrm{LC} \times \mathrm{VSR}$ $\begin{aligned} & =2 \mathrm{~cm}+(0.01) \times(2) \\ & =2.02 \mathrm{~cm}\end{aligned}$ Volume of sphere $=\frac{4}{3} \pi\left(\frac{d}{2}\right)^3=\frac{4}{3} \pi\left(\frac{2.02}{2}\right)^3$ $=4.32 \mathrm{~cm}^3$ Density $=\frac{\text { mass }}{\text { volume }}=\frac{8.635}{4.32}=1.998 \sim 2.00 \mathrm{~g}$

subject: Physics
q: While measuring diameter of wire using screw gauge the following readings were noted. Main scale reading is $1 \mathrm{~mm}$ and circular scale reading is equal to 42 divisions. Pitch of screw gauge is $1 \mathrm{~mm}$ and it has 100 divisions on circular scale. The diameter of the wire is $\frac{x}{50} \mathrm{~mm}$. The value of $x$ is :
question: While measuring diameter of wire using screw gauge the following readings were noted. Main scale reading is $1 \mathrm{~mm}$ and circular scale reading is equal to 42 divisions. Pitch of screw gauge is $1 \mathrm{~mm}$ and it has 100 divisions on circular scale. The diameter of the wire is $\frac{x}{50} \mathrm{~mm}$. The value of $x$ is :
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{MSR}=1 \mathrm{~mm}, \mathrm{CSR}=42, \text { pitch }=1 \mathrm{~mm} \\ & \mathrm{LC}=\frac{\text { pitch }}{\text { No. of CSD }}=\left(\frac{1}{100}\right)=0.01 \mathrm{~mm} \\ & \text { Diameter }=\mathrm{MSR}+\mathrm{LC} \times \mathrm{CSD} \\ & \text { Diameter }=1+(0.01) \times 42 \mathrm{~mm} \\ & \text { Diameter }=1.42 \mathrm{~mm}=\frac{\mathrm{x}}{50} \\ & \therefore \mathrm{x}=71\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q: In a screw gauge when the circular scale is given five complete rotations it moves linearly by $2.5$ mm. If the circular scale has $100$ divisions, the least count of screw gauge is _____ mm.
question: In a screw gauge when the circular scale is given five complete rotations it moves linearly by $2.5$ mm. If the circular scale has $100$ divisions, the least count of screw gauge is _____ mm.
solution:
Pitch of the screw gauge is the linear distance moved in one complete rotation.

$\text{Pitch} = \dfrac{2.5}{5} = 0.5 \text{ mm}$

Least count of the screw gauge is given by the ratio of the pitch to the total number of divisions on the circular scale.

$\text{Least count} = \dfrac{\text{Pitch}}{\text{Number of divisions on circular scale}}$

$\text{Least count} = \dfrac{0.5}{100} = 0.005 \text{ mm}$

$\text{Least count} = 5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ mm}$

Answer: $5 \times 10^{-3}$

subject: Physics
q:
A screw gauge gives the following reading when used to measure the diameter of a wire.
Main scale reading : $0 \mathrm{~mm}$
Circular scale reading : 52 divisions
Given that $1 \mathrm{~mm}$ on main scale corresponds to 100 divisions of the circular scale.
The diameter of wire from the above date is :
question:
A screw gauge gives the following reading when used to measure the diameter of a wire.
Main scale reading : $0 \mathrm{~mm}$
Circular scale reading : 52 divisions
Given that $1 \mathrm{~mm}$ on main scale corresponds to 100 divisions of the circular scale.
The diameter of wire from the above date is :
solution: Diameter of wire $=\frac{1}{100} \times 52=0.52 \mathrm{~mm}=0.052 \mathrm{~cm}$

Resonance is observed to occur at two successive lengths of the air column, <math><msub><mrow><mi>l</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>1</mn></mrow></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>30</mn><mtext> </mtext><mi mathvariant="normal">c</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> and <math><msub><mrow><mi>l</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>70</mn><mtext>  </mtext><mi mathvariant="normal">c</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo>.</mo></math> Then, <math><mi>ν</mi></math> is equal to:
question: A tunning fork of frequency <math><mn>480</mn><mi> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">z</mi></math> is used in an experiment for measuring speed of sound <math><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi>v</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> in air by resonance tube method. Resonance is observed to occur at two successive lengths of the air column, <math><msub><mrow><mi>l</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>1</mn></mrow></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>30</mn><mtext> </mtext><mi mathvariant="normal">c</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> and <math><msub><mrow><mi>l</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>70</mn><mtext>  </mtext><mi mathvariant="normal">c</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo>.</mo></math> Then, <math><mi>ν</mi></math> is equal to:
solution:
Speed of sound, v=2f l2-l1
=2×4800.70-0.30
=384 m/s

subject: Physics
q: In a screw gauge, 5 complete rotations of the screw cause it to move a linear distance of $0.25$ $\mathrm{cm}$. There are 100 circular scale divisions. The thickness of a wire measured by this screw gauge gives a reading of 4 main scale divisions and 30 circular scale divisions. Assuming negligible zero error, the thickness of the wire is:
question: In a screw gauge, 5 complete rotations of the screw cause it to move a linear distance of $0.25$ $\mathrm{cm}$. There are 100 circular scale divisions. The thickness of a wire measured by this screw gauge gives a reading of 4 main scale divisions and 30 circular scale divisions. Assuming negligible zero error, the thickness of the wire is:
solution:
Least count
$$
=\frac{\text { Value of } 1 \text { part on main scale }}{\text { Number of parts on vernier scale }}
$$
$$
=\frac{0.25}{5 \times 100} \mathrm{~cm}=5 \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{~cm}
$$
Reading $=4 \times 0.05 \mathrm{~cm}+30 \times 5 \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{~cm}$ $=(0.2+0.0150) \mathrm{cm}=0.2150 \mathrm{~cm}$ (Thickness of wire)

subject: Physics
q: In an experiment, a set of reading are obtained as follows - $1.24 \mathrm{~mm}, 1.25 \mathrm{~mm}, 1.23 \mathrm{~mm}$, 1.21 mm. The expected least count of the instrument used in recording these readings is $\_\_\_\_$ mm.
question: In an experiment, a set of reading are obtained as follows - $1.24 \mathrm{~mm}, 1.25 \mathrm{~mm}, 1.23 \mathrm{~mm}$, 1.21 mm. The expected least count of the instrument used in recording these readings is $\_\_\_\_$ mm.
solution:
Least count is the smallest division readable on an instrument scale.
Given readings are 1.24 mm, 1.25 mm, 1.23 mm, 1.21 mm, all expressed to two decimal places.
The precision of measurement shows differences as small as 0.01 mm (e.g., 1.25 - 1.24 = 0.01 mm).
This indicates all readings can be recorded to the nearest 0.01 mm, which represents the smallest measurable division.
Therefore, the least count of the instrument is 0.01 mm.

subject: Physics
q: <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn></math> divisions on the main scale of a Vernier calliper coincide with <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>11</mn></math> divisions on the Vernier scale. If each division on the main scale is of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn></math> units, the least count of the instrument is :
question: <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn></math> divisions on the main scale of a Vernier calliper coincide with <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>11</mn></math> divisions on the Vernier scale. If each division on the main scale is of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn></math> units, the least count of the instrument is :
solution:
Given:

10 MSD=11 VSD

⇒1 VSD=1011 MSD

LC=1MSD-1VSD

=1 MSD-1011 MSD

=1 MSD11

=511 units

subject: Physics
q: In a screw gauge, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn></math> complete rotations of the screw cause it to move a linear distance of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>25</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math>. There are<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>100</mn></math> circular scale divisions. The thickness of a wire measured by this screw gauge gives a reading of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn></math> main scale divisions and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>30</mn></math> circular scale divisions. Assuming negligible error, the thickness of the wire is
question: In a screw gauge, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn></math> complete rotations of the screw cause it to move a linear distance of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>25</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math>. There are<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>100</mn></math> circular scale divisions. The thickness of a wire measured by this screw gauge gives a reading of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn></math> main scale divisions and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>30</mn></math> circular scale divisions. Assuming negligible error, the thickness of the wire is
solution:
The Least count=0.255×100 cm=5×10-4 cm.

Reading =4×0.05 cm+30×5×10-4 cm
              =0.2+0.0150 cm=0.2150 cm

subject: Physics
q: The resistance of the meter bridge AB in given figure is $4 \Omega$. With a cell of emf $\varepsilon=0.5 \mathrm{~V}$ and rheostat resistance $\mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{h}}=2 \Omega$ the null point is obtained at some point J. When the cell is replaced by another one of emf $\varepsilon=\varepsilon_{2}$ the same null point $\mathrm{J}$ is found for $\mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{h}}=6 \Omega$. The $\operatorname{emf} \varepsilon_{2}$ is:
question: The resistance of the meter bridge AB in given figure is $4 \Omega$. With a cell of emf $\varepsilon=0.5 \mathrm{~V}$ and rheostat resistance $\mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{h}}=2 \Omega$ the null point is obtained at some point J. When the cell is replaced by another one of emf $\varepsilon=\varepsilon_{2}$ the same null point $\mathrm{J}$ is found for $\mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{h}}=6 \Omega$. The $\operatorname{emf} \varepsilon_{2}$ is:
solution:
Given, Emf of cell, $\varepsilon=0.5 \mathrm{v}$ Rheostat resistance, $\mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{h}}=2 \Omega$ Potential gradient is

$\frac{d v}{d L}=\left(\frac{6}{2+4}\right) \times \frac{4}{L}$

Let null point be at $\ell \mathrm{cm}$ when cell of $\mathrm{emf} \varepsilon=0.5 \mathrm{v}$ is used.

thus $\varepsilon_{1}=0.5 \mathrm{~V}=\left(\frac{6}{2+4}\right) \times \frac{4}{\mathrm{~L}} \times \ell$...(i)

For resistance $\mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{h}}=6 \Omega$ new potential gradient is $\left(\frac{6}{4+6}\right) \times \frac{4}{L}$ and at null point

$\left(\frac{6}{4+6}\right)\left(\frac{4}{\mathrm{~L}}\right) \times \ell=\varepsilon_{2}$...(ii)

Dividing equation (i) by (ii) we get

$\frac{0.5}{\varepsilon_{2}}=\frac{10}{6}$ thus $\varepsilon_{2}=0.3 \mathrm{v}$

subject: Physics
q: A screw gauge with a pitch of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0.5 </mn><mi>mm</mi></math> and a circular scale with <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>50</mn></math> divisions is used to measure the thickness of a thin sheet of aluminium. Before starting the measurement, it is found that when the two jaws of the screw gauge are brought in contact, the <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mn>45</mn><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">t</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">h</mi></mrow></msup></math> division coincides with the main scale line and that the zero of the main scale is barely visible. What is the thickness of the sheet if the main scale reading is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.5 </mo><mi>mm</mi></math> and the <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mn>25</mn><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">t</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">h</mi></mrow></msup></math> division coincides with the main scale line?
question: A screw gauge with a pitch of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0.5 </mn><mi>mm</mi></math> and a circular scale with <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>50</mn></math> divisions is used to measure the thickness of a thin sheet of aluminium. Before starting the measurement, it is found that when the two jaws of the screw gauge are brought in contact, the <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mn>45</mn><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">t</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">h</mi></mrow></msup></math> division coincides with the main scale line and that the zero of the main scale is barely visible. What is the thickness of the sheet if the main scale reading is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.5 </mo><mi>mm</mi></math> and the <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mn>25</mn><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">t</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">h</mi></mrow></msup></math> division coincides with the main scale line?
solution:
LC=0.550=0.01 mm

Zero error =0.50-0.45=-0.05

Thickness =0.5+25×0.01+0.05

=0.50+0.25+0.05

=0.80 mm

q:
A spectrometer gives the following reading when used to measure the angle of a prism.
Main scale reading: $58.5$ degree
Vernier scale reading : $09$ divisions
Given that $1$ division on main scale corresponds to $0.5$ degree. Total divisions on the vernier scale is $30$ and match with $29$ divisions of the main scale. The angle of the prism from the above data
question:
A spectrometer gives the following reading when used to measure the angle of a prism.
Main scale reading: $58.5$ degree
Vernier scale reading : $09$ divisions
Given that $1$ division on main scale corresponds to $0.5$ degree. Total divisions on the vernier scale is $30$ and match with $29$ divisions of the main scale. The angle of the prism from the above data
solution:
$\mathrm{L.C} =\frac{1}{60}$
Total Reading $=585+\frac{9}{60}=58.65$

subject: Physics
q: When two resistance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>R</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>R</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> connected in series and introduced into the left gap of a meter bridge and a resistance of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi></math> is introduced into the right gap, a null point is found at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>60</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> from left side. When <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>R</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>R</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> are connected in parallel and introduced into the left gap, a resistance of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi></math> is introduced into the right-gap to get null point at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>40</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> from left end. The product of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>R</mi><mn>1</mn></msub><mo> </mo><msub><mi>R</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> is ______ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></math>
question: When two resistance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>R</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>R</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> connected in series and introduced into the left gap of a meter bridge and a resistance of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi></math> is introduced into the right gap, a null point is found at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>60</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> from left side. When <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>R</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>R</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> are connected in parallel and introduced into the left gap, a resistance of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi></math> is introduced into the right-gap to get null point at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>40</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> from left end. The product of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>R</mi><mn>1</mn></msub><mo> </mo><msub><mi>R</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> is ______ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></math>
solution:
A meter bridge consists of a wire of length 1 m and of uniform cross-sectional area stretched taut and clamped between two thick metallic strips bent at right angles with two gaps across which resistors are to be connected. It works on the principle of the Wheatstone bridge. Therefore, we can write

R1+R210=6040⇒R1+R2=15 Ω

Then for the second case, we can write 

R1R2R1+R2×3=4060⇒R1R2=30 Ω2

subject: Physics
q: Least count of a vernier caliper is $\frac{1}{20 \mathrm{~N}} \mathrm{~cm}$. The value of one division on the main scale is $1 \mathrm{~mm}$. Then the number of divisions of main scale that coincide with $\mathrm{N}$ divisions of vernier scale is :
question: Least count of a vernier caliper is $\frac{1}{20 \mathrm{~N}} \mathrm{~cm}$. The value of one division on the main scale is $1 \mathrm{~mm}$. Then the number of divisions of main scale that coincide with $\mathrm{N}$ divisions of vernier scale is :
solution:
Least count of vernier calipers $=\frac{1}{20 \mathrm{~N}} \mathrm{~cm}$
$\because$ Least count $=1 \mathrm{MSD}-1 \mathrm{VSD}$
let $\mathrm{x}$ no. of divisions of main scale coincides with $\mathrm{N}$ division of vernier scale, then
$\begin{aligned} & 1 \mathrm{VSD}=\frac{\mathrm{x} \times 1 \mathrm{~mm}}{\mathrm{~N}} \\ & \therefore \frac{1}{20 \mathrm{~N}} \mathrm{~cm}=1 \mathrm{~mm}-\frac{\mathrm{x} \times 1 \mathrm{~mm}}{\mathrm{~N}} \\ & \frac{1}{2 \mathrm{~N}} \mathrm{~mm}=1 \mathrm{~mm}-\frac{\mathrm{x}}{\mathrm{N}} \mathrm{mm} \\ & \mathrm{x}=\left(1-\frac{1}{2 \mathrm{~N}}\right) \mathrm{N} \\ & \mathrm{x}=\frac{2 \mathrm{~N}-1}{2}\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q: If the screw on a screw-gauge is given six rotations, it moves by <math><mn>3</mn><mi></mi><mi>m</mi><mi>m</mi></math> on the main scale. If there are <math><mn>50</mn></math> divisions on the circular scale the least count of the screw gauge is:
question: If the screw on a screw-gauge is given six rotations, it moves by <math><mn>3</mn><mi></mi><mi>m</mi><mi>m</mi></math> on the main scale. If there are <math><mn>50</mn></math> divisions on the circular scale the least count of the screw gauge is:
solution:
Pitch =36=0.5 mm
L.C. =0.550 mm=1100 mm=0.1 mm=0.001 cm

subject: Physics
q:
In an experiment to find out the diameter of wire using screw gauge, the following observation were noted:

(a) Screw moves 0.5 mm on main scale in one complete rotation
(b) Total divisions on circular scale =50
(c) Main scale reading is 2.5 mm
(d) 45th  division of circular scale is in the pitch line
(e) Instrument has 0.03 mm negative error Then the diameter of wire is :
question:
In an experiment to find out the diameter of wire using screw gauge, the following observation were noted:

(a) Screw moves 0.5 mm on main scale in one complete rotation
(b) Total divisions on circular scale =50
(c) Main scale reading is 2.5 mm
(d) 45th  division of circular scale is in the pitch line
(e) Instrument has 0.03 mm negative error Then the diameter of wire is :
solution:
From the data given in the question,

Pitch of the screw gauge=0.5 mm.

Total divisions on the circular scalen=50.

Therefore, the least count of the screw gauge is LC=pitchn=0.550=0.01 mm

Now, MSR=2.5 mm, CSR=45

Diameter reading =MSR+LC×CSR- zero error

⇒d=2.5+0.45--0.03

⇒d=2.98 mm

Shown in the figure below is a meter-bridge set up with null deflection in the galvanometer.
The value of the unknown resistor $R$ is
question:

The value of the unknown resistor $R$ is

$$
\frac{55}{20}=\frac{R}{80} \Rightarrow R=\frac{55 \times 8}{2}=220 \Omega
$$

subject: Physics
q: One main scale division of a vernier callipers is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>a</mi></math> <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>cm</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>n</mi><mi>th</mi></msup></math> division of the vernier scale coincide with <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mfenced><mrow><mi>n</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></mfenced><mi>th</mi></msup></math> division of the main scale. The least count of the callipers in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>mm</mi></math> is :
question: One main scale division of a vernier callipers is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>a</mi></math> <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>cm</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>n</mi><mi>th</mi></msup></math> division of the vernier scale coincide with <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mfenced><mrow><mi>n</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></mfenced><mi>th</mi></msup></math> division of the main scale. The least count of the callipers in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>mm</mi></math> is :
solution:
n-1a=na'

a'=n-1an

∴ L.C.=1MSD-1VSD

=a-a' cm

=a-n-1an

=na-na+an=an cm

=10an mm

subject: Physics
q: Two full turns of the circular scale of a screw gauge cover a distance of $1 \mathrm{~mm}$ on its main scale. The total number of divisions on the circular scale is 50 . Further, it is found that the screw gauge has a zero error of $-0.03 \mathrm{~mm}$ while measuring the diameter of a thin wire, a student notes the main scale reading of $3 \mathrm{~mm}$ and the number of circular scale divisions in line with the main scale as 35 . The diameter of the wire is
question: Two full turns of the circular scale of a screw gauge cover a distance of $1 \mathrm{~mm}$ on its main scale. The total number of divisions on the circular scale is 50 . Further, it is found that the screw gauge has a zero error of $-0.03 \mathrm{~mm}$ while measuring the diameter of a thin wire, a student notes the main scale reading of $3 \mathrm{~mm}$ and the number of circular scale divisions in line with the main scale as 35 . The diameter of the wire is
solution: Diameter $=$ M.S.R. $+$ C.S.R $\times$ L.C. $+$ Z.E. $=3+35 \times(0.5 / 50)+0.03=3.38 \mathrm{~mm}$
